coredata.dc:title: Integrative role of PEDOT: PSS in adjusting the photoresponse efficiency of novel reduced graphene quantum dots/silicon heterojunction for optoelectronics and solar energy conversion applications
coredata.dc:creator: El-Mahalawy, Ahmed M.
Abbas, W.
Mostafa, Ola
Zidan, Nehad A.
Issa, Hanady Hussein
Fedawy, M.
Wassel, Ahmed R.
coredata.dc:description: The urgent need to realize high-efficiency solar cells and photodetectors for commercially available production, considering the cheap price of their preparation, inspired us to apply quantization technology to graphene particles. Therefore, we reported here for the first time manipulating and boosting the photoelectrical performance of the designed rGO-QDs/p-Si heterojunction using PEDOT:PSS interlayer. The structural properties of the employed rGO-QDs (average size ∼ 2 nm) and PEDOT:PSS thin films have been investigated individually using XRD, Raman, and TEM, while the optical properties have been spectrophotometrically inspected. The values of the energy gap and dispersion parameters of each film have been estimated. Toward exploiting the prepared devices in solar cell applications, after analysis of the measured electrical properties, we concluded that the presence of the PEDOT:PSS layer between the two layers of the device led to a significant improvement in the photovoltaic characteristics with Voc ∼ 0.807 Vs, Jsc ∼ 25.76 mA/cm2, FF ∼ 42.49 %, and PCE ∼ 8.83 %. In addition, on the scale of photodetection capability, the PEDOT:PSS modified device achieved outstanding performance with high responsivity (0.84 A/W), external quantum efficiency (196 %), detectivity (1.62 × 1011 cm.Hz0.5/W) and fast on/off switching behavior of 23/15.2 ms as rise/fall time. Such findings reveal an original indirect scenario about the superior photovoltaic and photodetection behavior of the implemented devices suggesting them strongly for efficient optoelectronics and solar cell applications.

head.ce:title: Integrative role of PEDOT: PSS in adjusting the photoresponse efficiency of novel reduced graphene quantum dots/silicon heterojunction for optoelectronics and solar energy conversion applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The urgent need to realize high-efficiency solar cells and photodetectors for commercially available production, considering the cheap price of their preparation, inspired us to apply quantization technology to graphene particles. Therefore, we reported here for the first time manipulating and boosting the photoelectrical performance of the designed rGO-QDs/p-Si heterojunction using PEDOT:PSS interlayer. The structural properties of the employed rGO-QDs (average size ∼ 2 nm) and PEDOT:PSS thin films have been investigated individually using XRD, Raman, and TEM, while the optical properties have been spectrophotometrically inspected. The values of the energy gap and dispersion parameters of each film have been estimated. Toward exploiting the prepared devices in solar cell applications, after analysis of the measured electrical properties, we concluded that the presence of the PEDOT:PSS layer between the two layers of the device led to a significant improvement in the photovoltaic characteristics with Voc ∼ 0.807 Vs, Jsc ∼ 25.76 mA/cm2, FF ∼ 42.49 %, and PCE ∼ 8.83 %. In addition, on the scale of photodetection capability, the PEDOT:PSS modified device achieved outstanding performance with high responsivity (0.84 A/W), external quantum efficiency (196 %), detectivity (1.62 × 1011 cm.Hz0.5/W) and fast on/off switching behavior of 23/15.2 ms as rise/fall time. Such findings reveal an original indirect scenario about the superior photovoltaic and photodetection behavior of the implemented devices suggesting them strongly for efficient optoelectronics and solar cell applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Future threats include the elevating global temperature, the dwindling supply of fossil fuels, and the ever-increasing demand for energy, all of which will necessitate scientists' and researchers' ongoing efforts to create efficient and affordable renewable energy alternatives. Photovoltaic systems are used increasingly frequently for generating electrical energy, but there are still challenges and impediments that need to be overcome before they can significantly contribute to fulfilling the global energy demand [1]. The previous few decades have witnessed a major evolution in solar cells, which have been divided into three generations to accommodate the many different technologies involved. Recently, the global solar energy society has also begun to promote the idea of a fourth generation. As the first generation of solar cells, monocrystalline and polycrystalline silicon cells were introduced that depend on the photogeneration of charge carriers in the bulk absorber assisted by built-in potential separation. These cells, however, struggled with the expensive cost of silicon fabrication as well as the limited size of the silicon wafers [1,2]. The second generation of solar cells, which are represented by cells made of thin films, overcame the first generation's exaggeration which has included cells based on cadmium telluride (CdTe) [3], gallium arsenide (GaAs) [4], copper-zinc-tin-selenium/sulfur (CZTS) [5], copper-indium-gallium-selenium/sulfur (CIGS) [6], and heterojunction with an intrinsic interlayer (HIT) [7]. Improved photoconversion efficiency (PCE) as well as low fabrication costs were the goal for the third generation of solar cells. Organic solar cells [8], dye-synthesized solar cells (DSSCs) [9], quantum dots dye-synthesized solar cells (QD-DSSCs) [1,10], perovskite cells [11], and multi-junction cell [12] types emerged throughout the third generation. These cells are considered excitonic-based solar cells that generate weakly coulombic bounded electron-hole (e-h) pairs, where their dissociation takes place owing to their band offsets at the interface. It is worth mentioning that the offset of HOMO and LUMO bands of both donor and acceptor played a significant role in controlling the performance of the heterojunction organic solar cells [13], besides the proper choice of the HTL and ETL [14]. In addition, plasmonic nanostructured particles have been extensively for improving the performance of organic solar cells via improving the light-harvesting capability of the absorbing layer by different mechanisms such as hot electron injection, enhancing the local field which facilitates the separation of charge carriers, light scattering, and resonant energy transfer by dipole-dipole coupling [15]. Besides, the fourth generation of solar cells has manifested as a hybrid structure of organic materials and inorganic nanoparticles [16].

In this essence, it should be mentioned that hybrid organic/inorganic heterojunction solar cells offer better photovoltaic characteristics owing to the strong built-in potential that can successfully separate the photogenerated excitons. In addition, the flexible nature, low temperature, and low fabrication cost owing to the solution-based fabrication approaches increase the favorability of hybrid organic/inorganic heterojunction solar cells [17]. However, the large number of organic materials (oligomers and polymers) validated for photovoltaic applications and among all the recorded organic/inorganic heterojunction solar cells, the combination of poly(3,4-ethylenedioxy-thiophene): poly(styrene-sulfonate) / silicon (PEDOT:PSS/Si) heterojunction incontrovertibly attracted an intense focus for optoelectronics and energy conversion applications [18–28]. PEDOT:PSS is an optically transparent, relatively conductive, facile processable, good passivator for silicon, and high work function (5.0–5.4 eV) p-type conjugated polymer [25,29]. Nevertheless, the large difference in work function between silicon and PEDOT:PSS results in high Schottky barrier height which causes a deficiency in its photovoltaic performance owing to the e-h recombination and limits its popularization on the market scale [25]. Additionally, among the reasons that may also lead to an efficiency deterioration and shortening the long-term stability in such cells is the conductivity dependence of hole transport effectiveness of the PEDOT:PSS, in addition to being a super-absorption material because of its containment of sulfonic acid, which may change the wettability of the silicon surface, which in turn alters the contact quality [26].

Manipulating the PEDOT:PSS/Si performance can be achieved either by enhancing the conductivity of PEDOT:PSS or engineering the PEDOT:PSS/Si interface. The conductivity improvement of PEDT:PSS has been extensively studied using solvent treatments or composite formation with other conductive materials. The conductivity improvement process depends on the addition of a polar solvent and surfactants such as dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethylene glycol (EG), N,N-dimethyl formamide (DMF), acetonitrile, and sorbitol which induces conformational changes of the PEDOT chains resulting in enhanced inter/intra-charge carriers’ mobility [19,20,25,28,29]. For the PEDOT:PSS/n-Si solar cells, interface engineering is crucial because it facilitates transportation and augments the separation of charge carriers' and lowers the interface recombination velocity [26]. The manipulation of the PEDOT: PSS/Si interface has been achieved throughout many approaches, for example, structuring the Si-surface via texturing, etching, formation of nanowires, or nanopillars for suppressing light reflections and enhancing the light trapping capability [22,26].

It is worth mentioning that since the discovery of graphene and as the result of its intriguing physical/chemical properties, unremitting efforts have been spurred, which in turn was reflected in the significant enhancement of a wide range of opto-(electronic) and solar conversion devices [30–33]. Regarding the photovoltaic performance of PEDOT:PSS, graphene (Gr) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) played a pivotal role in improving the efficiency of such cell architecture. Sharma et al. achieved a 45.3 % enhancement of Ag/PEDOT:PSS/n-Si by introducing GO (0.03 wt. %) to the PEDOT:PSS [34], while Trinh et al. employed GO and AuNP-decorated GO for improving the conductivity of PEDOT:PSS and therefore the cell performance increased from 5.81 % to 7.58 % and 8.39 %, respectively [35]. Moreover, the addition of 0.1 wt % of rGO to PEDOT:PSS enhanced the cell's PCE from 0.00014 % to 0.003 % as discussed by Behura's group [36]. In addition, incorporating Gr to the PEDOT:PSS layer with silicon nanowires (SiNWs) of length 400 nm achieved PCE of about 9.05 % which is 21.5 % better than planar Si/PEDOT:PSS-Gr [36]. Meanwhile, the efficiency of Ag/SiNWs/PEDOT:PSS has been enhanced from 0.1 % to 0.22 % after adding 0.01 mg rGO to PEDOT:PSS by Swain and his co-workers [17]. On the other side, interlayering PEDOT:PSS at the Gr/Si interface achieved PCE enhancement from 0.74 % to 1.58 % as introduced by Xie et al. [37]. Likewise, dopamine@graphene interlayered film at PEDOT:PSS/Si interface improved the cell's antireflectivity and enhanced its efficiency from 11.06 % to 13.15 % [38]. Lu et al. fabricated core/shell nanowire structured hybrid cells and achieved PCE of about 6.35 % [39].

Consequently, based on what has already been discussed, we are attempting in the current research to develop reduced graphene oxide quantum dots to utilize them in the fabrication of an organic/inorganic heterojunction for photodetection and solar cell applications. Then, for the designed device, we are planning to investigate how adding a passivation interlayer of PEDOT:PSS could influence photodetection and solar cell performance. In addition, through the current study, the optical and structural properties of PEDOT:PSS and rGO-QDs films are systematically examined individually. The insertion of PEDOT:PSS in the Au/rGO-QDs/p-Si architecture significantly improved the photodetection and power conversion efficiency which suggests the present devices as a promising architecture for reliable and efficient solar cell applications.

Reduced Graphene Oxide Quantum Dots (rGO-QDs) have been prepared using citric acid as a carbon source. Briefly, 5 g of citric acid (99.5 %) was melted at 180 °C for 12 h, 1.5 M solution of NaOH was added dropwise to the melted dense solution of citric acid at room temperature, and finally, hydrothermal treated at 180 °C for additional 12 h. The resultant dark brown powder was separated from the liquor via dialysis filtration, washed, and dried in a vacuum oven at 70 °C overnight [48].

Probe-sonication, the proper and simple physical method used to reduce GO [49,50], was done according to the following procedure. First, suspension aqueous colloids of GO were prepared from the dried GO by mechanical stirring and heat treatment at 60 °C. 1 g of GO was stirred into 100 ml of distilled water in the experiment. This dispersion was stirred using a Fisher mechanical stirring until it became a clear solution with no visible particulate material. After that, probe-sonicated for 20 min in pulsed mode (operating at an amplitude of 40 %) (20 KHz), then dried in a wide glass dish in a vacuum oven at 80 °C overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thin films and devices fabrication procedures
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 0.1 g of the synthesized rGO-QDs powder was suspended in deionized (DI) water after magnetic stirring for 0.5 h followed by sonication for 1 h. Individually, the thin films of rGO-QDs and PEDOT:PSS solution of 50 ML purchased from Sigma Aldrich have been deposited using a spin coating technique under 3000 rpm for 30 s and annealed at 125 °C for 15 min in ambient conditions. The fabricated rGO-QDs and PEDOT:PSS have been deposited on quartz substrates for optical investigations and on glass substrates for structural analyses. For the heterojunction fabrication, under the same conditions, rGO-QDs film has been spin-coated on pre-cleaned and cured 500 μm thick p-type silicon of orientation 〈100〉 and annealed at 125 °C for the device symbolized (D1). Whereas, for the second device of the PEDOT:PSS interlayer, firstly the PEDOT:PSS layer has been deposited on the silicon surface by spin coating at the same conditions and followed by depositing the rGO-QDs layer. The tri-layered configuration has been annealed at 125 °C for 15 min. Then a 90 nm thick finger-typed gold (Au) top-electrode and 300 nm thick aluminum (Al) rear electrode were deposited using an Edwards 306A coating unit under vacuum pressure ∼ 3 × 10−5 mbar. The resultant active area was about 0.03 cm2.

The crystal structure of the prepared films has been inspected using the D8 ADVANCE Bruker X-ray diffractometer-based XRD technique, while the microstructure and particle size of the synthesized rGO-QDs have been evaluated using HR-TEM Model JEM 2100, JEOL, Japan. In addition, Raman spectra of the rGO-QDs and PEDOT:PSS films have been recorded using a confocal Raman microscope (Witec Alpha 300 RA, 514 nm excitation). Besides, the UV–Vis-NIR (190–2500) nm transmittance and reflectance spectra of the prepared films have been studied using a Jasco V-770 double-beam spectrophotometer at room temperature. Electrically, the photodetection performance has been assessed using the measured current-voltage relations under dark and green laser illumination (532 nm) in the bias range of ±5 V. Meanwhile, the solar cell characteristics have been investigated under halogen source illumination of controllable intensities in the range of (20–100) mW/cm2 using IL1400A radiometer/photometer.

Fig. 1 (a) declares the XRD diffractogram of the synthesized rGO-QDs film with a broad intense peak at 2θ=20.8° of interlayer d-spacing 4.27 Å which is significantly higher than the well-known value of pristine graphite (3.35 Å). This highlights the role of the still existing oxidation functionalities (i.e., C–OH, C–OC, and O–C = O) on the graphene layers that assisted the expansion of the graphitic stack even after the eliminating of oxygen functional groups by reduction [51–53]. The estimated mean crystallite size value using the Scherrer equation was about 1.13 nm. On the other side, PEDOT:PSS XRD pattern shown in Fig. 1(b) shows a low-intensity broad peak at 2θ=24.5° of d-spacing of 3.63 Å, which is related to the (020) plane of PEDOT:PSS backbone [54,55].

The size uniformity of the synthesized rGO-QDs has been evaluated by analyzing the captured TEM micrographs revealed in Fig. 2 (a) and (b) at different magnifications. Uniform and homogeneously distributed particles of a size distribution shown in Fig. 2(c) of average particle size ∼ 2 ± 0.7 nm are clearly observed confirming the quantum dot configuration.

Molecularly, the Raman spectrum of the fabricated rGO-QDs thin film is visualized in Fig. 3 (a) with its conventional defect/disorder peak (D peak) at 1357.2 cm−1 and graphite peak (G peak) at 1594.6 cm−1. The D peak as the breathing mode of A1g symmetric K-point phonons is related to various types of local defects and disorders that may exist in the synthesized rGO-QDs such as crystallographic randomness or edge plane defects and disordered sp3 regions of carbon because of the destructive/reconstructive role of oxygen functional groups (i.e., C–OH, C–OC, C = O, and O–C = O) on the graphitic lattice. Whereas the in-plane stretching vibrations and E2g phonon of sp2 hybridized carbon are represented in the observed G peak [45,51,56]. Furthermore, the detected (D + G) peak at 2974 cm−1 is attributed to the carbon framework graphitization which agrees literature's Raman spectra of rGO-QDs [56]. The intensity ratio of D/G peaks (ID/IG) is about 0.82 which is relatively smaller than those were estimated previously in other reports confirming the high quality of the present form of rGO-QDs owing to the low density of defects which may be attributed to the reconstructed sp2 carbon atoms removing the functional groups containing oxygen [51,56].

The obtained values of La, LD, nD , and L sp 2 are about 20.45 nm, 12.45 nm, (2.03 × 1011) cm−2, and 20.18 nm, respectively.

Meanwhile, the Raman spectrum of PEDOT:PSS showed in Fig. 3(b), illustrates two fitted bands at 1372 cm−1 and 1583 cm−1 which are attributed to the inter-ring stretching vibration of thiophene Cβ-Cβ and asymmetric stretching vibration of thiophene Cα=Cβ, respectively [57–59]. The PSS contribution may be overlapped with Cα=Cβ at band 1583 cm−1 [60]. Basically, the charge carrier concentration in PEDOT:PSS depends on the ratio between its two tautomeric forms (e.g., quinoid and benzoid) [58]. Always benzoid component contributions appear in the high-frequency region while the quinoid component contributions arise in the low-frequency region of the Raman spectra. The frequency shifts may be related to the twist of some thiophene backbone, besides the intrachain linearity and interchain packing which results in an enhanced conjugation which in turn induces a wider Raman band profile [59].

The optical characteristics of the employed rGO-QDs and PEDOT:PSS thin films have been identified using the measured UV–Vis-NIR transmission (T) and reflection (R) spectra of both, as shown in Fig. 4 (a) and (b), respectively. On the one side, rGO-QDs thin film possessed semi-transparency (T ∼ 50 %) along the visible and near-infrared spectral ranges reaching a sharp fundamental absorption edge at 298 nm. In addition, it exhibited low reflectivity of (R ∼ 5 %) along the whole spectral ranges. These features are appropriate to be integrated as a layer of efficient heterojunction photoconversion devices, where the low reflectivity minimizes the reflected light intensity from the device's surface and the semi-transparency increases the transmitted light rays reaching the depletion region resulting in a higher density of electron-hole pairs. On the other side, PEDOT:PSS thin film exhibited high NIR-Vis transparency (70 % < T < 80 %) followed by a sharp fundamental edge at 295 nm. Likewise, PEDOT:PSS film possessed low reflectivity (R < 10 %) along the UV–Vis-NIR spectral regions. The higher transparency of PEDOT:PSS in the visible region and the relatively higher NIR reflectance is ascribed to the contribution of the generated polarons and free charge carriers owing to the strong attraction of sulfonate group of the PSS to the electrons of PEDOT chains suppression of the exciton absorption features of PEDOT:PSS in the visible region. Owing to the free charge carriers’ effect, the optical transmission increases monotonically as the wavelength decreases from 2500 nm to 800 nm, while in contrast, the optical reflection behaves inversely with the decrement of wavelength [58,61].

Accordingly, the absorption coefficient (α) of the prepared films of a thickness (d) have been calculated using the measured reflection and transmission spectra using the following relation:

The absorption characteristics of the deposited rGO-QDs shown in Fig. 5 (a) illustrate that the prepared film exhibits strong UV absorption with a wide absorption band which has been deconvoluted into three Gaussian components at energies 4.34 eV (286 nm), 4.63 eV (268 nm), and 5.46 eV (227 nm) and an extended visible band tail. The characteristic absorption peak of rGO has been detected at 268 nm [62]. Meanwhile, regarding the GO absorption features, the absorption peak that is observed at 228 nm is related to the π-π* transition of the confined sp2 system in the C = C bond [62,63], while the absorption peak at 286 nm is assigned to the n-π* transitions in the C = O of oxygen-bearing functional groups [62,64]. The noticeable high absorption coefficient of rGO-QDs reaches (1.6 × 105 cm−1) is ascribed to the recovered high sp2 electronic conjugation after reduction, besides the quantum size effect which induces discretization of energy levels results in higher absorption cross-section. The continuous absorption features of rGO-QDs are attributed to the electronic transition bands resulting not only from the closely-spaced electronic energy levels but also the vibronic coupling. Likewise, the broad intense UV absorption band of PEDOT:PSS film declared in Fig. 5(b) has been deconvoluted in four absorption peaks at 5.93 eV (209 nm), 5.04 eV (246 nm), 4.58 eV (271 nm), and 4.48 eV (277 nm). Since all the PSS absorption features always do not appear in energies lower than 4 eV, all the detected peaks arise from the aromatic rings of PSS [61,65]. The polaron/bipolaron subgap transitions (BPT) absorption contributions of PEDOT are weakly detected at the inset of Fig. 5(b) in the low energy region [58,61].

Among the outstanding features of rGO-QDs is the modulation of its HOMO/LUMO energy gap (Eg ), which can be altered by either the surface/edge chemical functionalization or the lateral size [66,67]. Overcoming the graphene's metallic nature and linear dispersion relation (Eg ∼ 0 eV), which causes excessively large dark currents in the photodetection devices, and uncontrollable charge carriers dynamics and limits its applicability in optoelectronics can be achieved by means of quantum dots which allows the opening of the band gap owing to the quantum confinement and edge effects [66]. The estimated value of the indirect energy gap of the synthesized rGO-QDs thin films using the Tauc method shown in Fig. 5(c) is about 3.85 eV. Regarding the size dependence of Eg , the estimated value of Eg is comparable to what was spectroscopically estimated (3.8 eV [44]) and theoretically derived for GQDs (3.83 eV [66]), and GQDs of 96 carbon atoms and 120 total atoms in the model structure (3.78 eV [68]). Although increasing the reduction of graphene oxide may finely control the energy gap of graphene (i.e., energy gap values exponentially decay as the oxygen vacancies increase in the rGO-QDs structure [69]), the highly reduced GO suffers opaqueness causing a parasitic absorption, which limits its applicability in optoelectronics, especially in heterojunction devices and multilayered devices. Hence, quantum dots offer a versatile approach for fine-controlling energy gaps without diminishing their transparency. On the other side, PEDOT:PSS thin film exhibited a close value of indirect energy gap of about 3.66 eV as declared in Fig. 5(d) to the previously reported values (3.62 eV [61]). This convergence in the Eg values between rGO-QDs and PEDOT:PSS may be one of the most important reasons for the compatibility between them when combined in a heterojunction configuration and facilitates the process of transporting charges and reduces the possibilities of recombination.

The real part of the refractive index of both films shown in Fig. 6 (a) and (b) reveals a normal dispersion behavior beyond 700 nm, which is explainable in terms of the single oscillator model and anomalous dispersion in the UV–Vis regions which is attributed to the multi-oscillator's hypothesis. Meanwhile, the absorption index (i.e., extinction coefficient) of both is illustrated as a function of wavelength in Fig. 6(c), and (d) emphasizes the UV absorption features besides the growing increment with increasing the wavelength, especially in the NIR region. This observation for PEDOT:PSS is attributed to the role of free charge carriers’ absorption, and polaron/bipolaron transitions [58,61]. Whereas the elevation of the extinction coefficient of rGO-QDs at higher wavelengths (i.e., NIR) has been previously reported for reduced graphene oxide and graphene but always lower than the values at low wavelengths (i.e., UV) [71–73]. Excitingly, here the k-values in the UV and NIR regions are considerably comparable which may be ascribed to free carriers’ absorption owing to the recovered sp2 configuration in addition to the quantum confinement effect arising from the quantum dot structure which caused energy levels discretization resulting in the absorption of low energetic photons which increases its appropriateness for wide-band photodetection and solar cell applications [74].

Following the criteria of Wemple and DiDomenico single oscillator model [75], the dispersion parameters (e.g., dispersion energy Ed , and oscillator energy Eo ) of such thin films can be extracted in the normal dispersion region by applying the following relation [70]:

where εL is the lattice dielectric constant, e is the absolute value of the electron charge, εo electric permittivity of free space, c is the speed of light, and λ is the wavelength. The estimated values of N/m* and εL from the linear fitting of (n 2-k 2) against λ 2 relation depicted in Fig. 7(c) and (d) are about 1.204 × 1056 kg−1m−3 and 2.201 for rGO-QDs, and 3.485 × 1055 kg−1m−3 and 2.663 for PEDOT:PSS thin films, respectively. The very high value of N/m* of rGO-QDs film coincident with the argument of free carriers’ absorption in the NIR region. The relatively small charge carriers’ concentration of PEDOT:PSS may confirm that polaron/bipolaron transitions absorption is the dominating reason for the transparency in the NIR region.

As illustrated in Fig. 7(e) and (f) for rGO-QDs and PEDOT:PSS thin films, respectively, the values of So and λo have been determined. The estimated oscillator wavelength of both films lies in the visible region, while the oscillator strength values of both films are close.

For the solar cell applicability test, two configured devices have been implemented; one consists of rGO-QDs spin-coated layer over p-type silicon sandwiched between two electrodes Al and Au designated as D1, and in the second configured device D2 an interlayer of PEDOT:PSS has been added as explained in Fig. 8 . The measured dark current-voltage (I-V) relations of the fabricated devices have been plotted in a semilogarithmic representation as revealed in Fig. 9 . Both the fabricated devices exhibited rectifying Schottky behavior with a linear incremental behavior at low forward bias, which is followed by exponential trend in the high bias region, which is attributed to the series resistance of the devices. A quasi-static voltage independence behavior of the reverse current of both devices is highlighted. Obviously, the insertion of PEDOT:PSS as an interlayer in D2 induced suppression of the dark current on both forward and reverse conditions. The microelectronic parameters of the implemented devices such as ideality factor (n), reverse saturation current (Io ), and barrier height (Φ B ) have been extracted using a thermionic emission model at V > 3kBT/q to fit the dark I-V relation as follows [36,76]:

where the diode terminal voltage is represented by Vd = (V-IRs ), kB represents Boltzmann's constant, q gives the electronic charge, and T is the temperature. The reverse saturation current and the barrier height in the absence of series resistance are interrelated by the following relation [36,76]:

The ideality factor, reverse saturation current, and barrier height were estimated from the linear fitting of the logarithmic current-voltage relation in the low forward bias region. As illustrated, the insertion of PEDOT:PSS interlayer in the device architecture boosted the junction quality by improving the ideality factor from 3.30 to 2.79 by manipulating the interfacial surface of the fabricated junction. The nonideal behavior of the designed devices (n > 1) is ascribed to the recombination currents in the space charge region [77]. The estimated values of the ideality factor are better than those reported previously for PEDOT:PSS/Si heterojunctions [18,20,77]. The PEDOT:PSS layer passivated the silicon surface by suppressing the dangling bonds and enhanced the contact of rGO-QDs/p-Si since the rGO-QDs and PEDOT:PSS can be molecularly bonded in several pathways (e.g., hydrogen bonding with PSS, electrostatic and π-π bonding with PEDOT) [34]. Therefore, the reverse saturation current reduces from 1.19 μA to 0.12 μA after PEDOT:PSS insertion referring to the elongation of the length needed for travel by minority carriers relative to their diffusion length. Besides, the barrier height has been elevated from 0.646 eV to 0.704 eV owing to the interfacial layer distinguishable role, which may result in a better photovoltaic effect with higher PCE owing to the strengthened internal field required for charge separation [45]. The existence of PEDOT:PSS elevated the barrier for minority carriers owing to the EC-LUMO and Ev-HOMO offsets [78]. This may result in a low recombination of the charges and hence lower reverse saturation current [23]. The significance of the PEDOT:PSS role on the designed devices’ performance has been observed in the calculated values of the rectification ratio (RR) of both devices as shown in Fig. 10 . The obvious difference between both devices in rectification is ascribed to the role of PEDOT:PSS in passivating the silicon surface, minimizing the band offsets between molecular orbitals (HOMO/LUMO) of rGO-QDs and valance/conduction bands of silicon, besides forming double internal built-in fields at the two junction PEDOT:PSS/p-Si and PEDOT:PSS/rGO-QDs. This may result in hindering the e-h recombination and facilitates the charge carrier's transportation owing to the remarkable conductivity of PEDOT:PSS [48].

The response of the designed devices D1 and D2 for halogen illumination of different intensities has been tested as shown in Fig. 11 (a) and (b), respectively. For D1, the illumination intensity variation induced a monotonic increase of the device's current especially under the reverse bias side with a small value of open circuit voltage (Voc ) owing to the photovoltaic effect that arises from the e-h generation, splitting, and collection with the help of the initiated built-in potential. The reverse current under the illumination of D1 becomes slightly voltage dependent as declared in Fig 12 (a) owing to the recombination between electrons and holes which needed reverse bias for widening the depletion region and increasing the built-in field to be effective for efficient splitting and collection of the photogenerated e-h pairs. On the other side, D2 responded effectively to the illumination intensity variations with higher values of open circuit voltage and high stability of reverse current under illumination as shown in Fig 12(b) confirming the successful passivation of silicon surface with the help of PEDOT:PSS and the effective generation/splitting/collection of the charge carriers.

The influence of the illumination intensity variation on the parasitic resistances of the designed solar cells including series resistance (Rs ) and parallel shunt resistance (Rsh ) has been visualized in Fig. 14 (a) and (b). The values of Rs and Rsh have been calculated from the negative reciprocated slope of J-V relations at V=Voc and J=Jsc conditions, respectively. The series resistance of D1 related to rGO-QDs has been increased from 8.463 kΩ to 23.59 kΩ upon increasing the illumination intensity from 20 to 100 mW/cm2 which may be related to the high density of photogenerated charge carriers which accumulates near the junction and causes their losses elevating the series resistance. After filling the localized trapping states, the rest of these free charges can recombine together easily owing to the trivial role of the built-in potential which cannot successfully separate and collect them. But in D2 the incorporation of PEDOT:PSS interlayer resulted in a significant decrease of the series resistance from 116.5 kΩ to 96.15 Ω owing to the improved photoconductivity of the device and limited leakage current. Meanwhile, the shunt resistance of both devices descended gradually as the illumination intensity increased. Despite, the decrement of such shunt resistance under high illumination intensities causing degradation of Voc , the elevation of Voc because of the high density of the photogenerated electrons and holes yields a stable balanced behavior as observed in Fig. 14(c). Besides, the small Io of D2 compared to D1 enhances the Voc values of D2 rather than D1, where the reverse saturation current density (Jo =Io / A) and open circuit voltage are interrelated as follows [17,79]:

The small discrepancy between the measured and calculated values of open circuit voltage may be ascribed to the unconsidered localized trapping states arising from the surface defects and unsaturated bonds of silicon which activates the recombination process and hence reduces Voc values [25,45,79]. Additionally, the short circuit current density has been increased remarkably with increasing the illumination intensity as depicted in Fig. 14(d) with an enhancement of about 4584 % of Jsc at 100 mW/cm2 when the PEDOT:PSS layer has been inserted into the device architecture. The high Jsc of D2 compared to D1 is related to the estimated low reverse saturation current (Io ), which indicates the small loss of the photogenerated charge carriers by recombination [48,77]. It is worth noting that, the optical losses owing to the surface reflectivity of D1 (R ∼ 21.45 %) are higher than D2 (R ∼ 18.15 %) owing to the difference between the estimated refractive indices of PEDOT:PSS and rGO-QDs from the optical characterization section considering silicon's refractive index. This can be interpreted as follows when the light rays incident with definite incidence angle from air to rGO-QDs then to p-Si in the designed D1 device, the refraction angle of the refracted ray will be minimized yielding a total short optical path when the ray reflects from the rear electrode surface generating low density of e-h pairs. Whereas, in device D2, the proportion of light transmitted is high and the total thickness of the whole device is higher than D1 providing a higher optical path for the refracted rays since rGO-QDs/PEDOT:PSS layers act as a light-trapping layer. Therefore, resulting in a higher density of photogenerated e-h pairs yielding a higher Jsc . The reason why D1 recorded small Voc and high Io is the lowered barrier owing to the image force effects, and the superficial silicon oxide layer's impedance [44].

Considering the fill factor (FF) and power conversion efficiency (PCE) as figures of merit of any solar cell, the values of FF and PCE of the present devices have been calculated as follows [78]:

where A is the active area of the fabricated cell, and Pin is the intensity of the incident illumination. The dependence of FF and PCE of the designed cells on the incident illumination intensity has been illustrated in Fig. 14(e) and (f), respectively. The FF decrement of D1 agrees with the observed increment of series resistance as the incident intensity increases owing to the trapping of the photogenerated charge carriers [21]. This is evident in the interrelation between series resistance and fill factor is expressed as FF=FFo (1-rs ) [1–1/rsh ], where the normalized series resistance (rs =Jsc . Rs /Voc ), and (rsh =Voc /Jsc . Rsh ) as discussed in the diode model. The FF enhancement of D2 with the elevation of illumination intensity is a sufficient indicator for the efficient excitons photogeneration and the proper e-h splitting and collection. Besides, the power conversion efficiency has been improved from 0.021 % to 8.84 % under 100 mW/cm2 after introducing the PEDOT:PSS layer in the cell architecture. The calculated cell performance output parameters are listed in Table 1 compared to other organic/inorganic heterojunction solar cells that comprise Gr, rGO, and PEDOT:PSS in different structures. Despite, the wide and intense spectral absorption profile of the synthesized rGO-QDs film, the designed solar cell D1 achieved poor performance with PCE ∼ 0.042 % which may be ascribed to the low charge carrier mobility in QDs architecture which hinders the effective spatial separation and transport of the photogenerated e-h pairs. Excitingly, as shown in the aforementioned table, manufactured planar cells showed superior and distinctive performance and outperformed the performance of many other organic/Si heterojunction-based solar cells. It should be mentioned that theoretically, n-type graphene/p-type silicon solar cell performance has been investigated in architecture ITO/n-Gr/p-Si/Ag and achieved PCE of about 7.623 % employing 40 layers of n-Gr [80].

Regarding the band diagram of device D1 illustrated in Fig. 15 (a), under illumination electron-hole pair can be formed either owing to the absorption of rGO-QDs or/and p-Si which can be splitted owing to the interfacial strong built-in potential. The generated holes can be easily injected from HOMO of rGO-QDs to the valance band of p-Si and hence to Al rear electrode, while the generated electrons suffer a high potential barrier between the conduction band of silicon and LUMO of rGO-QDs, then it prefers motion toward Al rear electrode giving rise to a high recombination probability at the rear electrode yielding a poor photovoltaic performance [44,81]. But the insertion of the PEDOT:PSS layer as an interlayer between rGO-QDs and p-Si facilitated the holes transportation by lowering the potential barrier for holes (EV -HOMO) between silicon and PEDOT:PSS, while the potential barrier for electrons has been increased confirming the electron-blocking mechanism from p-Si to rGO-QDs, as explained in the suggested band diagram in Fig. 15(b). However, it should be mentioned that photogenerated electrons in PEDOT:PSS could be easily transported to rGO-QDs owing to the low barrier between them.

Fig. 16 (a) and (b) reveal the I-V curves of the designed devices D1 and D2, respectively, under dark and laser illumination of intensity 10 mW/cm2 for the photodetection (PD) applicability test. Each of the two prepared devices showed high sensitivity to incident light in both forward/reverse directions with slight Voc (∼ 0.15 V) for D2. The elevation of the devices’ current under illumination is ascribed to not only the photocurrent generated by the direct photoexcitation of charges generating e-h pairs but also the charge multiplication by impact ionization [83,84]. Multiple e-h generations take place by single photon owing to long-lived hot electrons because the relaxation caused by charge-charge scattering in graphene occurs significantly more quickly than that caused by charge-phonon scattering [84]. The variation of the photocurrent (Iphoto = Iillumination - Idark ) of both devices as a function of bias voltage shown in Fig. 17 (a) signifies the superiority of D1 's photocurrent rather than D2 under high bias voltages (V > −1.89 volt), upon increasing the reverse bias from −1.89 to -5 volt, the photocurrent difference (Iphoto
-D1 - Iphoto
-D2) has been improved from −1.2 μA to +0.228 μA. This may be explained as follows: from the optical characterization section, the penetration depth (1/α) at λ =532 nm for rGO-QDs and PEDOT:PSS is about 887 nm and 108.7 nm, respectively. This would result in an effective reach of optical rays to the rGO-QDs/p-Si depletion region yielding efficient generation and splitting of the e-h pairs in D1. But the high thickness of the D2 device may not guarantee the proper reach of optical rays to the interface depletion region, decreasing the effective generation and splitting of the e-h pairs resulting in a lower photocurrent. However, under illumination at room temperature and low reverse bias (V < −1.89 volt) the rGO-QDs exhibit negative photoconductivity (NPC) behavior (i.e., conductivity decreases) because of the adsorbed oxygen and water molecules on its surface, therefore the photocurrent of D1 is lowered [85]. However, the existence of PEDOT:PSS in D2 compensated for the photoconductivity deterioration of the device as a whole and enhanced photocurrent slightly by developing a conductive path for charge transfer [86].

Indeed, for quantifying the performance of the implemented PDs, responsivity (i.e., intensity ratio between photocurrent density and incident light intensity) (Rλ ) has been calculated and plotted as shown in Fig. 17(b) revealing the same trend of photocurrent variation. The estimated responsivities of D1 and D2 at −3 Vs are about 1.6 A/W and 0.84 A/W, respectively. The estimated responsivity values are higher than those of InGaAs and Si PDs (∼ 0.9 and 0.5 A/W, respectively) and surpass most of the GQDs based PDs in prior reports (∼ 0.5 A/W [83], ∼ 34 mA/W [87], 0.56 A/W [88]), and PEDOT:PSS based PDs (∼ 0.230 A/W [89]). While these values are lower than those describing some of the high-performance PDs based on N-doped GQDs (∼ 1.06 × 106 A/W [74]), 2578 A/W [90]), and PDs based on AuNPs@GQDs (103 A/W [91]). The external quantum efficiency (EQE) of the PDs is related to responsivity as follows [86,88]:
(13)

× 100 ,
where h is the Planck constant. Fig. 17(c) shows the bias voltage dependence of quantum efficiency of the fabricated PDs which has been significantly increased with increasing bias voltage for both PDs. The significant photocurrent gain of the fabricated PDs has been observed from exceeding the EQE 100 %, which is related to the photomultiplication of charges owing to hot charges of rGO-QDs by impact ionization [83,84,89]. This high efficiency increases the potentiality of the present PDs for high-efficiency real applications [89]. The high gain photodetection performance behavior has been observed in prior reports related to QDs-based PDs [88,92].

Furthermore, to index the performance of the constructed PDs under weak optical signals, and considering the dominance of shot noise mechanism rather than flicker and thermal noise sources, the specific detectivity (D*) and noise equivalent power (NEP) are interrelated as follows [76,83,93]:

where Jdark is the dark current density, and Δf is the electrical bandwidth. The specific detectivity has been enhanced from 7.7 × 1010 to 1.62 × 1011 cm.Hz0.5/W upon inserting PEDOT:PSS to the bare PD architecture because of the suppressed dark current as explained in Fig. 17(d). It is worth clarifying that, as the noise level of PDs is maintained as low as possible, the photodetection performance becomes more precise. The noise current (IN ) is interrelated to the minimum detectable power (NEP) as (IN NEP. Rλ ) [83,93]. Fig. 17(e) and (f) illustrated that PD-D2 achieved the lowest noisy performance with NEP and IN at −5 V of about 1.07 pW/Hz0.5 and 0.88 pA, respectively, while PD-D1 achieved NEP and IN at −5 V about 2.19 pW/Hz0.5 and 3.55 pA, respectively. Besides, the strength of the generated electrical signal to the noise signal of the designed PDs has been estimated in terms of signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) as follows [89,94]:

,

Successfully, reduced graphene oxide quantum dots (rGO-QDs) have been synthesized to be exploited in heterojunction solar cells and photodetectors. Firstly, the structural investigations for each rGO-QDs and PEDOT:PSS individually confirmed the QD-architecture with a particle size of about 2 nm for rGO and confirmed their structural stability after deposition. Besides, the optical investigations unveiled the high UV absorption features of each film with an energy gap of about 3.85 and 3.66 eV for rGO-QDs and PEDOT:PSS, respectively. On the scale of solar cell applications inserting PEDOT:PSS layer between rGO-QDs and p-Si significantly improved the quality of the junction and suppressed the dark yielding better photovoltaic performance with PCE of about 8.83 %, which is considered a new record in rGO-QDs-based heterojunction solar cells. Nonetheless, for photodetection applications PEDOT:PSS interlayered rGO-QDs/p-Si device achieved better performance with outstanding figures of merit and remarkable charge multiplication behavior (EQE >100 %). Futuristically, we aim to improve the photovoltaic performance of this architecture by employing textured silicon to increase light absorption and decrease the silicon's reflection. In addition, we are going to use Ag-NPs and Au-NPs to enhance the photodetection performance depending on the surface plasmonic features of the metal nanoparticles.
